questions.question: What is a computer network?
questions.explanation: A collection of autonomous computers linked to exchange resources.

questions.question: Which network spans a city?
questions.explanation: MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) covers a metropolitan area like a city.

questions.question: What is the smallest network type?
questions.explanation: PAN (Personal Area Network) is for personal devices like phone/watch.

questions.question: The Internet is an example of which network?
questions.explanation: WAN (Wide Area Network) spans global distances.

questions.question: What is a 'node'?
questions.explanation: Any device (PC, server, printer) in a network is a node.

questions.question: Which network is localized in a single building?
questions.explanation: LAN (Local Area Network) is for homes, offices, and schools.

questions.question: What is ARPANET?
questions.explanation: The ancestor of the modern internet, started by DARPA.

questions.question: What does NIC stand for?
questions.explanation: Also called a Network Interface Unit (NIU), it links the PC to the medium.

questions.question: What is 'interoperability'?
questions.explanation: The ability of diverse hardware/software to communicate using standards.

questions.question: Which provides the highest speed?
questions.explanation: LANs offer superior bandwidth due to short cable distances.

questions.question: What is 'Topology'?
questions.explanation: The physical or logical layout of devices in a network.

questions.question: Which topology has a central hub/switch?
questions.explanation: All nodes connect directly to a central controller.

questions.question: Which topology uses a single backbone cable?
questions.explanation: Bus topology uses a single common path called the trunk.

questions.question: Which topology is most fault-tolerant?
questions.explanation: Mesh provides redundant paths between every node.

questions.question: What is a 'Hub'?
questions.explanation: Hubs repeat signals to all ports, making them inefficient.

questions.question: What is a 'Switch'?
questions.explanation: Switches use MAC addresses to send data only to the correct destination.

questions.question: Which device connects two different networks?
questions.explanation: Routers handle pathfinding between heterogeneous networks (e.g. LAN to WAN).

questions.question: What does a Modem do?
questions.explanation: MOdulator-DEModulator translates between binary and carrier signals.

questions.question: What is a 'Repeater'?
questions.explanation: Repeaters regenerate signals to overcome attenuation over long distances.

questions.question: What is a 'Gateway'?
options: Entry point for networks with different protocols
questions.explanation: Gateways translate between different architectural standards.

questions.question: What is 'Bandwidth'?
questions.explanation: The capacity of a communication channel in bits per second.

questions.question: Which is guided (wired) media?
questions.explanation: Coaxial cables use physical copper paths.

questions.question: Which is unguided (wireless) media?
questions.explanation: Microwaves travel through the atmosphere without wires.

questions.question: Which medium uses light for transmission?
questions.explanation: Total Internal Reflection in glass strands allows light travel.

questions.question: Which twisted pair is better shielded?
questions.explanation: Shielded Twisted Pair reduces electromagnetic interference.

questions.question: What is 'Attenuation'?
questions.explanation: The weakening of energy as a signal travels further.

questions.question: Which frequency does Satellite communication use?
questions.explanation: High-frequency microwaves (GigaHertz) are needed to reach orbit.

questions.question: What is the standard unit for data rate?
questions.explanation: Bandwidth is measured in bps, Kbps, Mbps, or Gbps.

questions.question: Which medium is immune to EMI?
questions.explanation: Glass doesn't conduct electricity, so it ignores magnetic interference.

questions.question: What is 'Crosstalk'?
questions.explanation: Coupling of signals between parallel transmission lines.

questions.question: How many layers does the OSI model have?
questions.explanation: The OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) model has 7 layers.

questions.question: Which layer handles physical transmission of data?
questions.explanation: Layer 1 (Physical Layer) deals with cables, signals, and hardware.

questions.question: At which layer does MAC addressing operate?
questions.explanation: Layer 2 (Data Link Layer) uses MAC addresses for local communication.

questions.question: Which layer handles IP addresses?
questions.explanation: Layer 3 (Network Layer) manages IP routing and logical addressing.

questions.question: Layer 4 in the OSI model is also called:
questions.explanation: Layer 4 (Transport Layer) handles TCP, UDP, and end-to-end delivery.

questions.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for encryption?
questions.explanation: Layer 6 (Presentation Layer) handles encryption and compression.

questions.question: At which layer do HTTP, FTP, and SMTP operate?
questions.explanation: Layer 7 (Application Layer) is where user applications and protocols reside.

questions.question: Which layer ensures reliable data delivery?
questions.explanation: Layer 4 (Transport Layer) ensures reliable packet delivery through TCP.

questions.question: What does Layer 5 (Session Layer) manage?
questions.explanation: Session Layer establishes, maintains, and terminates communication sessions.

questions.question: Data units at Layer 3 are called:
questions.explanation: Packets are the data units used at the Network Layer (Layer 3).

questions.question: How many layers does the TCP/IP model have?
questions.explanation: The TCP/IP model has 4 layers (also called DoD model).

questions.question: Which protocol ensures reliable delivery of data?
questions.explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides connection-oriented, reliable delivery.

questions.question: UDP stands for:
questions.explanation: UDP provides fast, connectionless data transmission without reliability checks.

questions.question: Which protocol is used for web browsing?
questions.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the foundation of the World Wide Web.

questions.question: What does SMTP handle?
questions.explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is used for sending emails.

questions.question: DNS translates:
questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) converts domain names like google.com to IP addresses.

questions.question: Which protocol is used for file transfer?
questions.explanation: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) enables secure file transfer between systems.

questions.question: ICMP is primarily used for:
questions.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) handles ping and traceroute commands.

questions.question: Which port does HTTP use by default?
questions.explanation: HTTP operates on port 80; HTTPS uses port 443 for secure communication.

questions.question: POP3 and IMAP are protocols for:
questions.explanation: Both POP3 and IMAP are email retrieval protocols used by clients.

questions.question: How many bits does an IPv4 address have?
questions.explanation: IPv4 addresses consist of 32 bits, written as 4 octets (e.g., 192.168.1.1).

questions.question: How many bits does an IPv6 address have?
questions.explanation: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits, providing vastly more addresses than IPv4.

questions.question: What is the range of values in each octet of IPv4?
questions.explanation: Each octet represents 8 bits, ranging from 0 to 255 (2^8 - 1).

questions.question: What is a subnet mask?
options: Identifies network and host portions of IP
questions.explanation: Subnet masks determine which portion of an IP address is the network versus host.

questions.question: The default subnet mask for Class A is:
questions.explanation: Class A uses /8 subnet mask: 255.0.0.0, allowing 16.7 million hosts.

questions.question: What is CIDR notation?
questions.explanation: CIDR (Classless Inter-Domain Routing) notation like 192.168.1.0/24 specifies network size.

questions.question: A /24 subnet mask in dotted decimal is:
questions.explanation: /24 means 24 bits for network, 8 for hosts = 255.255.255.0.

questions.question: How many usable hosts are in a /25 subnet?
questions.explanation: /25 has 8 bits for hosts; 2^8 = 256, minus 2 (network & broadcast) = 254 usable IPs.

questions.question: Which IP range is reserved for private networks (Class A)?
questions.explanation: All three ranges are private: 10.x.x.x, 172.16.x.x-172.31.x.x, 192.168.x.x.

questions.question: What is the broadcast address in 192.168.1.0/24 subnet?
questions.explanation: The broadcast address has all host bits set to 1 (last address in subnet).

questions.question: What is the difference between switching and routing?
options: Switching is for LANs, routing for WANs
questions.explanation: Switching operates within a single network (L2), routing between different networks (L3).

questions.question: What is MAC address learning in switches?
options: Dynamic mapping of MAC to port
questions.explanation: Switches learn source MAC addresses and remember which port they arrived on.

questions.question: What is broadcast domain?
options: Network where broadcast reaches all devices
questions.explanation: A broadcast domain is a segment where broadcast traffic reaches all connected devices.

questions.question: Routers separate:
questions.explanation: Routers create separate broadcast domains, limiting unnecessary broadcast traffic.

questions.question: What is VLAN?
questions.explanation: VLAN allows logical grouping of devices regardless of physical location.

questions.question: Which routing protocol is classful?
questions.explanation: RIPv1 (Routing Information Protocol v1) is a classful routing protocol.

questions.question: OSPF stands for:
questions.explanation: OSPF is a link-state routing protocol used for dynamic routing.

questions.question: What is a routing table?
options: List of known networks and next hops
questions.explanation: A routing table contains entries mapping destination networks to next-hop gateways.

questions.question: What metric does RIP use for routing?
questions.explanation: RIP uses hop count (number of routers) with a maximum of 15 hops.

questions.question: BGP is used for:
questions.explanation: BGP (Border Gateway Protocol) handles routing between Autonomous Systems on the Internet.

questions.question: What is a firewall?
questions.explanation: Firewalls monitor and control incoming/outgoing network traffic based on rules.

questions.question: What is a DMZ?
questions.explanation: DMZ is a buffered zone between internal network and the Internet for public-facing services.

questions.question: What does encryption do?
questions.explanation: Encryption converts plaintext to ciphertext using cryptographic algorithms.

questions.question: What is a VPN?
questions.explanation: VPN creates encrypted tunnel for secure communication over untrusted networks.

questions.question: Which encryption type uses two keys?
questions.explanation: Asymmetric encryption uses public and private key pairs (RSA, ECC).

questions.question: What is authentication?
questions.explanation: Authentication confirms that a user or device is who/what it claims to be.

questions.question: What does SSL/TLS provide?
questions.explanation: SSL (Secure Sockets Layer) / TLS provide encryption for web and email protocols.

questions.question: What is a proxy server?
questions.explanation: Proxy servers filter requests, cache content, and provide anonymity.

questions.question: What does IDS stand for?
questions.explanation: IDS monitors network traffic for malicious patterns or policy violations.

questions.question: What is a DDoS attack?
options: Multiple sources flooding target with traffic
questions.explanation: Distributed Denial of Service attacks overwhelm targets from many sources simultaneously.

questions.question: What is DHCP?
questions.explanation: DHCP automatically assigns IP addresses and network configuration to devices.

questions.question: What is the purpose of NAT?
options: Translating private IPs to public IPs
questions.explanation: NAT (Network Address Translation) allows multiple devices to share one public IP.

questions.question: What does SNMP monitor?
questions.explanation: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) collects data from network devices.

questions.question: What is NTP used for?
questions.explanation: NTP (Network Time Protocol) ensures accurate time across network devices.

questions.question: What does ARP do?
questions.explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) maps IP addresses to MAC addresses.

questions.question: What is LDAP used for?
options: Lightweight Directory Access Protocol for user/device info
questions.explanation: LDAP stores and retrieves directory information like user accounts and devices.

questions.question: What is a DNS zone?
questions.explanation: DNS zone is a portion of domain namespace for which a nameserver is authoritative.

questions.question: What does a secondary DNS server do?
options: Backup copy of primary server records
questions.explanation: Secondary DNS provides redundancy by maintaining copies of primary DNS records.

questions.question: What is a proxy ARP?
options: Device responding to ARP on behalf of another
questions.explanation: Proxy ARP allows a device to respond to ARP requests for another device's IP.

questions.question: What is TTL in DNS?
options: Time To Live - how long DNS record is cached
questions.explanation: TTL determines how long a DNS response can be cached before re-querying.

questions.question: What is QoS?
questions.explanation: QoS mechanisms prioritize critical traffic (VoIP, video) over less important data.

questions.question: What is Load Balancing?
questions.explanation: Load balancing distributes network traffic across multiple resources to optimize utilization.

questions.question: What is the OSI model's role in networking?
options: Reference framework for network communication standards
questions.explanation: OSI model provides standardized framework helping vendors design interoperable products.

questions.question: What is encapsulation in networking?
options: Wrapping data with headers at each layer
questions.explanation: Each OSI layer adds its own header; called encapsulation (headers called PDU).

questions.question: What is MTU?
options: Maximum Transmission Unit - largest packet size
questions.explanation: MTU (typically 1500 bytes for Ethernet) limits packet size; larger packets are fragmented.

questions.question: What is network congestion?
questions.explanation: Congestion occurs when network demand exceeds available bandwidth, causing packet loss/delays.

questions.question: What is Latency?
questions.explanation: Latency measures the time delay from source to destination (measured in milliseconds).

questions.question: What is Jitter?
questions.explanation: Jitter is inconsistent latency causing variations in packet arrival times.

questions.question: What is the difference between hub and switch at the core?
options: Hub broadcasts all ports; switch uses MAC lookup
questions.explanation: Hubs flood all ports (L1); switches intelligently forward based on MAC (L2).

questions.question: What is network slicing?
options: Dividing network into isolated logical networks
questions.explanation: Network slicing creates independent virtual networks from shared physical infrastructure, key for 5G.
